title: practical english usage - Part 106
content:
In older English, the ^/-structure was more common in this case (e.g. a dress a silk dress a stone bridge an iron rod a gold ring Noun + noun is normally used to say what things are made of.

4 'made    of': a  silk    dress;     silken       skin sot a piece of paper (     a paper piece) pack (of cards), herd, flock, group and so on.

collections, like piece, slice, lump (of sugar), bunch (of flowers), blade (of grass), We   also prefer the oʃ-strueture with words that refer to units, selections and 3  units,    selections       and   collections:       piece,      group,       etc a box of matches a tin of paint a cup of coffee with its contents.

But we  use the preposition structure (with of) to talk about a container together ɑ  matchbox a paint tin a coffee cup Noun + noun is used for particular kinds of container.

2 containers:       a  matchbox;          a  box     of   matches not She was showing signs of tiredness, (     ... ihetlnt*^ signs:) - What does that road sign say?

Who's the girl in the comer? (     H7 /?   the corner girl?) corner tables.) - She was sitting at a comer table in the restaurant. (Restaurants often have not He was reading a book about the moon, (     a-tnoon-book) - He was reading a history book, (a common class of book) More examples: a man from the health department (not a regular kind of visitor) regularly at a British home) the postman, the milkman (well-known kinds of people who  may call cases we  prefer a preposition structure. Compare: well-known classes (so that the two nouns really describe a single idea). In other We   use noun + noun especially to talk about things that belong to common a sheepdog (a particular kind of dog) mineral water (a sort of water) mountain plants (a special group of plants) which  name a particular kind of thing.

The  noun + noun structure is mostly used to make ‘ classifying’  expressions, 1  classifying        expressions:        a  sheepdog preposition        structure?

126 classifying        expressions:        noun    + noun    or Section11 Nouns     and   Noun    Phrases;    Agreement grammar •  127   classifying expressions with 's: a child's toy; cow's milk not                                                                 notusually a table leg (     a table's leg) a car door (                 a car's door) structure.

But to talk about parts of non-living things, we  usually use the noun + noun ɑ  man's leg an elephant's trunk a sheep's heart We   use the 's structure to talk about parts of people's and animals' bodies.

3 parts:   a  man's    leg;   a  table      leg chicken soup a lamb chop    tortoise shell calf skin    chamois leather  fox fur When  the animal is killed to provide something, we  usually use noun + noun.

sheep's wool  a bird's egg  (    camel hair, horsehair) cow's milk   lamb's wool  a hen's egg The  's structure is often used for products from living animals.

2 noun    + 's + noun:   cow's     milk;    a  hen's     egg a doll's house (BrE)      a doll house (AmE) a baby's pram (BrE)        a baby carriage (AmE) a baby's bottle (BrE)        ɑ  baby bottle (AmE) British and American usage sometimes differ. Compare: baby clothes a birdcage Not all ‘ used by' expressions have possessive 's.

a child's toy children's clothes Generally, either both nouns are singular or both are plural.

women's magazines a bird's nest children's clothes            a man's sweater animal: the first noun refers to the user.

This is common when we  are talking about things that are used by a person or In some classifying expressions we  use a structure with possessive 's.

1 noun    + 's + noun:   children's         clothes;       a  bird's     nest cow's     milk 127 classifying        expressions         with    's: a  child's      toy; But wooden and woollen just mean 'made of wood/wool'.

a stone roof  a stony silence a lead pipe          a leaden sky (grey and depressing) silk stockings       silken skin a gold watch       golden memories made of, while the adjective has a more metaphorical meaning. Compare: different meanings. Generally the noun simply names the material something is A  few pairs of nouns and adjectives (e.g. gold, golden) are used as modifiers with grammar *128  singular expressions with plural verbs The team is in Detroit this weekend. They expect to win.           -> cases (though family can have a plural verb). Plural pronouns can be used.
